Questions.Title: What does SQL stand for?
Questions.QuestionText: What does the acronym SQL stand for?
Questions.Hint: Think about what language is used to communicate with relational databases.
Questions.Feedback: SQL stands for Structured Query Language, a standardized language used to communicate with and manipulate relational databases.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Structured Query Language
Sequential Question Logic
Standard Query Loop
Structured Question List

Questions.Title: Who formalized the relational database concept?
Questions.QuestionText: Who is credited with formalizing the concept of relational databases in 1970?
Questions.Hint: Think about the historical figure associated with the relational model.
Questions.Feedback: Edgar F. Codd formalized the relational concept in 1970, establishing the theoretical foundation for modern relational database systems.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Edgar F. Codd
Alan Turing
Charles Babbage
Tim Berners-Lee

Questions.Title: What is a primary key in a table?
Questions.QuestionText: What is the purpose of a primary key in a relational database table?
Questions.Hint: Consider how individual rows are distinguished from one another.
Questions.Feedback: A primary key is a special column or combination of columns that uniquely identifies each row in a table, ensuring no two rows share the same key value.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: A column or combination of columns that uniquely identifies each row
A column that stores the most important data in the table
A row that contains the column headers
A default value assigned to every new record

Questions.Title: What term describes rows in SQL terminology?
Questions.QuestionText: In relational database terminology, what alternative terms are used to refer to rows in a table?
Questions.Hint: Think about the technical vocabulary used alongside the word 'rows.'
Questions.Feedback: In relational database terminology, rows are also called 'records' or 'tuples.' Both terms are commonly used interchangeably with 'rows.'
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Records or tuples
Fields or attributes
Keys or indices
Schemas or views

Questions.Title: Purpose of the SELECT statement in SQL
Questions.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the SQL SELECT statement?
Questions.Hint: Think about which SQL command is used whenever you want to retrieve data.
Questions.Feedback: The SELECT statement is the foundational SQL command used to retrieve data from one or more database tables. Every data retrieval operation begins with SELECT.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: To retrieve data from one or more database tables
To insert new rows into a database table
To delete records from a database table
To create a new table in the database

Questions.Title: What does the asterisk wildcard do?
Questions.QuestionText: What does using the asterisk (*) in a SELECT query instruct the SQL engine to do?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens when you don't specify individual column names.
Questions.Feedback: The asterisk (*) is a wildcard character that instructs SQL to return every column in the specified table. For example, SELECT * FROM customers; returns all columns and all rows.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Return every column in the specified table
Return only the first row of the table
Multiply numeric values in the query
Search for records matching any text pattern

Questions.Title: Role of the WHERE clause in SQL
Questions.QuestionText: What does the WHERE clause do when added to a SELECT statement?
Questions.Hint: Think about how you narrow down the rows returned by a query.
Questions.Feedback: The WHERE clause filters rows based on one or more conditions, returning only records that meet the specified criteria. Without WHERE, a query returns every row in the table.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Filters rows based on one or more specified conditions
Sorts the result set in ascending order
Limits the number of rows returned to a fixed count
Specifies which columns to display in the output

Questions.Title: Correct syntax for filtering NULL values
Questions.QuestionText: Which syntax should be used to correctly filter rows where a column contains a NULL value?
Questions.Hint: Remember that NULL cannot be tested with standard comparison operators.
Questions.Feedback: NULL values require special handling. You must use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL when filtering for missing data. Writing WHERE column = NULL will never return results.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: WHERE phone_number IS NULL
WHERE phone_number = NULL
WHERE phone_number == NULL
WHERE phone_number EQUALS NULL

Questions.Title: How does BETWEEN work in SQL?
Questions.QuestionText: When using the BETWEEN operator in a SQL WHERE clause, are the boundary values included in the results?
Questions.Hint: Think about whether the endpoints of the range are part of the returned data.
Questions.Feedback: The BETWEEN operator is inclusive of both boundary values. For example, WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 50 returns products priced at exactly $10, exactly $50, and everything in between.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Yes, BETWEEN includes both boundary values
No, BETWEEN excludes both boundary values
Only the lower boundary value is included
Only the upper boundary value is included

Questions.Title: AND vs OR operator precedence in SQL
Questions.QuestionText: When combining AND and OR in a WHERE clause without parentheses, which operator does SQL evaluate first?
Questions.Hint: Think about which logical operator has higher precedence in SQL.
Questions.Feedback: AND is evaluated before OR in SQL. Using parentheses is recommended to explicitly control evaluation order and avoid unintended results when combining both operators.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: AND is evaluated before OR
OR is evaluated before AND
Both AND and OR are evaluated left to right equally
The database evaluates OR before AND by default

Questions.Title: Default sort order of ORDER BY
Questions.QuestionText: What is the default sort order when using ORDER BY without specifying ASC or DESC?
Questions.Hint: Think about which direction SQL sorts when no keyword is provided.
Questions.Feedback: By default, ORDER BY sorts results in ascending order (smallest to largest, A to Z, earliest to most recent). The ASC keyword can be added explicitly, but it is the default behavior.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Ascending order
Descending order
Random order
The order in which rows were inserted

Questions.Title: Using DESC in ORDER BY clause
Questions.QuestionText: What effect does adding the DESC keyword to an ORDER BY clause have on query results?
Questions.Hint: Think about how DESC changes the direction of sorting.
Questions.Feedback: The DESC keyword reverses the sort order, returning the largest or most recent values first. It is commonly used for ranking or finding top records, such as highest revenue products.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Results are sorted from the largest or most recent values first
Results are sorted from the smallest or earliest values first
Results are returned in the original insertion order
Duplicate rows are removed from the results

Questions.Title: Sorting by multiple columns in ORDER BY
Questions.QuestionText: When sorting by multiple columns in ORDER BY, how are the columns separated and can each have its own sort direction?
Questions.Hint: Consider the syntax used when listing more than one column in ORDER BY.
Questions.Feedback: Multiple columns in ORDER BY are separated by commas, and each column can independently have its own ASC or DESC direction to handle primary and secondary sorting.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Separated by commas; each column can have its own ASC or DESC
Separated by semicolons; all columns share the same sort direction
Separated by AND; only the first column can specify a direction
Separated by pipes; directions alternate automatically

Questions.Title: What does the LIMIT clause do?
Questions.QuestionText: What is the primary function of the LIMIT clause in a SQL query?
Questions.Hint: Think about how you control the number of rows returned from a large table.
Questions.Feedback: The LIMIT clause restricts the number of rows a SQL query returns, regardless of how many rows match the query conditions. It is useful for performance, sampling, and top-N queries.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Restricts the number of rows the query returns
Restricts the number of columns displayed
Sets a maximum value for a numeric column filter
Prevents duplicate rows from appearing in results

Questions.Title: LIMIT syntax in Microsoft SQL Server
Questions.QuestionText: Which syntax does Microsoft SQL Server use to limit the number of rows returned, instead of the LIMIT keyword?
Questions.Hint: Think about how SQL Server differs from MySQL or PostgreSQL for row restriction.
Questions.Feedback: Microsoft SQL Server uses TOP (n) placed after SELECT rather than a LIMIT clause. For example: SELECT TOP 10 * FROM customers; Oracle traditionally uses ROWNUM or FETCH FIRST n ROWS ONLY.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: TOP (n) placed after SELECT
LIMIT n placed at the end of the query
ROWCOUNT n placed before FROM
MAXROWS n placed after WHERE

Questions.Title: Correct SQL clause order for queries
Questions.QuestionText: What is the correct order of clauses when writing a SQL query that includes SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT?
Questions.Hint: SQL requires clauses to appear in a specific sequence to avoid syntax errors.
Questions.Feedback: The standard clause order is SELECT → FROM → WHERE → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Deviating from this order will cause a syntax error in most SQL databases.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: SELECT → FROM → WHERE → ORDER BY → LIMIT
SELECT → WHERE → FROM → LIMIT → ORDER BY
FROM → SELECT → WHERE → ORDER BY → LIMIT
SELECT → FROM → ORDER BY → WHERE → LIMIT

Questions.Title: Using LIKE for text pattern matching
Questions.QuestionText: Which SQL operator is used to perform pattern-based text matching in a WHERE clause?
Questions.Hint: Think about the operator that works with wildcard characters like the percent sign.
Questions.Feedback: The LIKE operator is used for pattern-based text matching. For example, WHERE last_name LIKE 'Sm%' returns records where the last name starts with 'Sm', such as Smith or Smart.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: LIKE
MATCH
CONTAINS
SIMILAR

Questions.Title: Using NOT operator in WHERE clause
Questions.QuestionText: What does the NOT operator do when used in a SQL WHERE clause?
Questions.Hint: Think about how NOT changes the logic of a condition.
Questions.Feedback: The NOT operator negates a condition, returning rows where the specified condition is FALSE. It can be combined with IN, LIKE, and BETWEEN to exclude categories or patterns.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: Negates a condition, returning rows where the condition is FALSE
Combines two conditions so both must be true
Returns rows where either of two conditions is true
Removes duplicate rows from the result set

Questions.Title: Combining ORDER BY with LIMIT for top-N queries
Questions.QuestionText: Which combination of SQL clauses is used to answer top-N business questions, such as identifying the five highest-spending customers?
Questions.Hint: Think about which clauses work together to rank and then restrict the output.
Questions.Feedback: Combining ORDER BY with LIMIT enables top-N queries. For example: SELECT customer_name, total_spent FROM customers ORDER BY total_spent DESC LIMIT 5; returns the five highest-spending customers.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: ORDER BY with DESC and LIMIT
WHERE with BETWEEN and GROUP BY
SELECT with DISTINCT and HAVING
FROM with JOIN and ASC

Questions.Title: Validating query results against business expectations
Questions.QuestionText: After running a SQL query, why should an analyst verify that the results make sense in the context of the original business question?
Questions.Hint: Think about the types of errors that syntax checks alone cannot catch.
Questions.Feedback: Validating results catches logical errors that syntax checks cannot detect. For example, if a query for top customers returns only one row when dozens are expected, the LIMIT value or WHERE condition may be too restrictive.
Questions.TopicTitle: Introduction to SQL and Relational Databases

Options.Text: To catch logical errors that syntax checks cannot detect
To confirm that the SQL engine compiled the query without warnings
To ensure the query runs faster on subsequent executions
To verify that the table has no duplicate primary keys
